_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology per Englund et al. NEJM 2008 (PMID 18784100). Surgical outcomes per Sihvonen et al. NEJM 2013 (PMID 24369076) FIDELITY trial. Treatment per Beaufils et al. EUSSKA consensus 2017 (PMID 28210785). Rehabilitation per Logerstedt et al. JOSPT 2018. Degenerative tear evidence per Katz et al. NEJM 2013.

h1Title: Meniscus Tear: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: A meniscus tear is a partial or complete rupture of one of the two C-shaped fibrocartilage disks that cushion the knee between the femur and tibia. Tears are among the most common knee injuries, with an estimated annual incidence of 60-70 per 100,000 person-years and prevalence rising sharply with age — MRI studies show meniscal tears in 35% of adults over 50, most of which are asymptomatic. Acute tears in younger athletes follow a twisting or pivoting injury on a planted foot, classically during football, basketball, soccer, or skiing, and present with sudden knee pain, swelling, and a sensation of locking or catching. Degenerative tears in middle-aged and older adults develop insidiously over months without a clear injury. Most stable, vertical tears in the peripheral (vascular) zone heal after meniscal repair with sutures; degenerative tears in middle-aged patients respond as well to structured physical therapy as to arthroscopic surgery, and surgical meniscectomy is now reserved for mechanical symptoms refractory to rehabilitation.

keyStats.prevalence: Lifetime incidence approximately 60-70 per 100,000 per year; MRI prevalence 35% in adults over 50
keyStats.demographics: Male-to-female ratio approximately 2.5:1 in acute traumatic tears; women predominate in degenerative tears after age 60
keyStats.avgAge: Peak acute tears age 15-35 years; degenerative tears 45-65 years
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 6-10 million meniscal injuries diagnosed annually worldwide; over 700,000 knee arthroscopies in the US in 2021 (AAOS)

definition: A meniscus tear (ICD-10: S83.20-S83.28 traumatic; M23.2 degenerative) is a disruption of the medial or lateral meniscus — two semilunar fibrocartilage structures that sit between the femoral condyles and tibial plateau in each knee. The menisci are 3-5 mm thick at their periphery, tapering centrally, and transmit roughly 50% of joint load when the knee is extended and up to 85% when flexed. They are vascularized only at the outer 25-33% (the red zone); the inner two-thirds (the white zone) rely on synovial diffusion and have limited healing capacity. Tear morphology defines management: vertical longitudinal tears (often radial or bucket-handle) in younger patients are amenable to repair; radial, oblique flap, and horizontal cleavage tears are common in older patients and are usually degenerative. The 2020 ISAKOS classification grades tear depth, location, and pattern to guide surgical planning. Meniscal injury also classifies by setting: acute traumatic (typically a pivoting injury on a planted foot, often with associated anterior cruciate ligament or medial collateral ligament injury); chronic; and degenerative (insidious onset without single injury, often associated with early osteoarthritis). Management is led by sports medicine physicians and orthopedic surgeons working with physical therapists, and the contemporary trend strongly favors meniscal preservation over excision to slow the progression to symptomatic osteoarthritis.

typesClassification.description: Tear runs along the long axis of the meniscus. Bucket-handle tears displace centrally into the intercondylar notch and cause mechanical locking. Most common in young athletes; in the red zone, they have a 70-90% healing rate after suture repair.

Tear extends from the inner free edge of the meniscus toward the periphery. Disrupts hoop tension and dramatically increases tibiofemoral contact pressure. Recent root-tear repair techniques aim to restore hoop function and slow osteoarthritis progression.

Splits the meniscus into upper and lower leaves. Often degenerative and associated with parameniscal cysts (especially lateral). Surgical options are limited to repair in younger patients or partial meniscectomy when mechanical symptoms persist.
Multiple tear patterns coexisting, usually with cartilage damage. Common in patients over 50. Modern guidelines recommend a 3-month trial of structured exercise therapy before considering surgery.

Avulsion of the meniscal attachment to the tibial plateau at the anterior or posterior horn. Functionally similar to total meniscectomy and accelerates osteoarthritis; transtibial pull-out repair restores hoop tension in selected patients.
Congenital disk-shaped (rather than semilunar) meniscus, almost always lateral, more prone to tearing in children and young adults. Treated with saucerization and repair where possible.

primarySymptoms: Sudden sharp pain at the joint line — medial pain for medial tears, lateral pain for lateral — typically after a twisting or pivoting injury on a flexed and planted knee.
Knee swelling that develops over 6-24 hours; bloody effusion within 2 hours suggests an associated anterior cruciate ligament injury rather than isolated meniscus tear.
Sensation of catching, clicking, or popping during knee flexion or rotation.
True mechanical locking (inability to fully straighten the knee) when a torn fragment displaces into the joint; characteristic of bucket-handle tears.
Pain on deep squatting, kneeling, or descending stairs.
Joint-line tenderness on palpation reproducing the patient's pain — the single most sensitive bedside sign (sensitivity 76%, specificity 29%).
Positive McMurray or Thessaly test with palpable click and reproducible pain.
Effusion of variable size; large effusions are uncommon in degenerative tears.
Quadriceps wasting visible after 2-4 weeks of altered weight-bearing.
Functional impairment with difficulty cutting, twisting, or returning to pre-injury sport.
earlyWarningSigns: A recent twisting injury during sport followed by knee pain and inability to continue play
New joint-line pain after a deep squat or sudden change of direction
Recurrent knee 'giving way' on uneven ground in a previously stable knee
Persistent knee effusion after a single injury that does not resolve over 7-10 days
Knee catching or transient locking lasting seconds during daily activity
emergencySigns: True locked knee unable to extend, indicating a displaced bucket-handle fragment — requires urgent orthopedic review for reduction
Knee dislocation with deformity, severe pain, or neurovascular compromise (pale, pulseless, or numb foot) — emergency department immediately
Acute knee with rapid hemarthrosis (tense effusion within 2 hours) suggesting associated ACL rupture or fracture
Severe joint pain with fever and warmth — exclude septic arthritis with urgent aspiration
New foot drop or calf numbness after a knee injury — concern for common peroneal nerve injury

causes.cause: Acute twisting injury on a planted foot
causes.description: Sudden rotation of the femur on a fixed tibia (cutting, pivoting, deceleration) compresses and shears the meniscus between the femoral and tibial condyles. Common in football, soccer, basketball, skiing, and tennis. Often associated with anterior cruciate ligament and medial collateral ligament injuries (the unhappy triad).

Repetitive or single-event deep squat with rotation can tear the posterior horn, especially of the medial meniscus. Occupational squatting (mining, carpet laying, agricultural work) accounts for a measurable share of medial posterior horn tears.

causes.cause: Degenerative wear in middle-aged and older adults
causes.description: Cumulative microtrauma and age-related fibrocartilage weakening produce horizontal cleavage and complex tears without identifiable injury. Almost universal by age 60 on MRI; symptomatic tears coexist with early osteoarthritis.

Up to 50-60% of acute ACL tears are associated with concurrent meniscal tear, most commonly the lateral meniscus at the time of injury and the medial meniscus in chronic ACL deficiency. Reconstruction restores knee stability and protects subsequent meniscal repair.

Approximately 3-5% of people have a discoid lateral meniscus, which is thicker and more prone to tearing. Symptoms (pain, snapping, locking) typically appear in childhood or adolescence. Saucerization with or without peripheral repair is the surgical treatment.

Tibial plateau fractures and direct blows to the flexed knee can tear menisci as part of the injury pattern. CT and MRI guide combined treatment, and the meniscus is often addressed at the time of fracture fixation.

riskFactors.description: Football, soccer, basketball, rugby, and skiing carry the highest meniscal injury rates. Targeted neuromuscular training programs (FIFA 11+, PEP) reduce knee injuries by 30-40% in randomized trials.
Degenerative tears become more common with each decade after 40; prevalence reaches 35% on MRI after 50 and over 50% by 70, although most are asymptomatic.
Men have approximately 2.5-fold higher incidence of acute meniscal tears, largely reflecting sports participation patterns. Degenerative tears affect women equally and more after age 60.
Obesity increases joint loading and is associated with degenerative meniscal tears and earlier osteoarthritis. Weight loss of 5-10% reduces knee pain and may slow further damage.
ACL deficiency increases shear forces across the menisci; chronic ACL injury leads to medial meniscus tears in 40-60% of untreated cases within 5 years.
Jobs requiring kneeling more than 1 hour daily double the risk of medial meniscal tear. Ergonomic adjustments and rotation of duties reduce occupational tear incidence.
Congenital variant present in 3-5% of people; produces snapping knee and predisposes to tearing in childhood and adolescence.

affectedDemographics: Annual incidence 60-70 per 100,000; up to 5-fold higher in athletes participating in pivoting sports
MRI prevalence 35% in adults over 50, mostly asymptomatic (Framingham study)
Male predominance approximately 2.5:1 in acute traumatic tears
Degenerative tears affect men and women roughly equally after age 60
Up to 50-60% of acute ACL ruptures have associated meniscal tear
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines a focused injury history with clinical examination and selective imaging. The history asks about the mechanism (twist, pivot, squat), timing of swelling (rapid versus delayed), mechanical symptoms (locking, catching, giving way), and prior knee injury. Examination starts with inspection and palpation for effusion, range of motion, joint-line tenderness, and Lachman/anterior drawer testing for associated ACL injury. Provocative meniscal tests include the McMurray test (knee fully flexed, then extended with internal and external rotation; positive on pain or click at the joint line), the Apley grind test (prone with knee flexed at 90 degrees, axial compression and rotation through the tibia), and the Thessaly test (single-leg stance at 20 degrees of flexion with internal and external rotation; sensitivity 64-66%, specificity 53-92%). Plain radiographs (standing AP, lateral, sunrise, Rosenberg view) exclude fracture and assess osteoarthritis but do not show meniscal tears. MRI is the imaging gold standard with sensitivity of 90-95% for medial and 80-85% for lateral tears; degenerative MRI changes must be interpreted with clinical findings to avoid attributing knee pain to incidental tears. Arthroscopy is the definitive diagnostic test when imaging is equivocal but is now uncommon as a purely diagnostic procedure. The 2018 ESSKA meniscus consensus recommends a structured framework combining symptom pattern, mechanical signs, and tear morphology to choose between repair, partial meniscectomy, and non-operative care.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical examination including joint-line tenderness, McMurray, Apley, and Thessaly tests
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies meniscal injury and helps distinguish from ACL, MCL, and patellofemoral pathology
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Conducted at the bedside in 5-15 minutes. The physician moves the knee through flexion, extension, and rotation; some maneuvers elicit pain or click, others test stability. Most are uncomfortable but brief.

diagnosticTests.test: Standing knee radiographs (AP weight-bearing, lateral, Rosenberg, sunrise)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes fracture, assesses joint-space narrowing and osteoarthritis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard X-rays taken while standing on the affected leg. Takes 10 minutes; results same day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms tear location, type, and extent; assesses adjacent ligament and cartilage damage
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minute scan in the MRI tunnel without contrast for most knee evaluations. Some patients receive intra-articular gadolinium for re-tear evaluation. Report within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive evaluation and simultaneous treatment of tear, cartilage, and other intra-articular pathology
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Outpatient surgical procedure under regional or general anesthesia. Two or three small incisions; recovery same day; physical therapy starts within 1 week.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects effusion, Baker's cyst, and parameniscal cysts; useful for guiding aspirations and injections
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Bedside scan of 10-15 minutes. Less sensitive than MRI for meniscal tears but useful in dynamic assessment and image-guided injection.

treatmentOverview: Management decisions hinge on tear pattern, location (red versus white zone), patient age, activity level, and presence of associated ligament or cartilage injury. For acute traumatic tears in younger patients with peripheral, vertical, or repairable patterns, all-inside or inside-out arthroscopic meniscal repair is the preferred approach because preserving meniscal tissue protects long-term cartilage and reduces osteoarthritis risk by approximately 30% versus meniscectomy at 10 years. Repair is performed within 6-12 weeks of injury when possible; root tears are repaired with transtibial pull-out techniques. Post-repair protocols restrict weight-bearing for 4-6 weeks and limit knee flexion beyond 90 degrees during the first 6 weeks. Acute peripheral tears in stable knees can also heal with conservative management (relative rest, physical therapy, and supervised return to activity over 6-12 weeks) when the patient prefers to avoid surgery. For degenerative tears in middle-aged and older adults, multiple randomized trials (FIDELITY, METEOR, MERIT, Sihvonen 2013) have shown structured exercise therapy is non-inferior to arthroscopic partial meniscectomy at 6, 12, and 24 months, so 3-6 months of physical therapy is recommended before considering surgery. Surgical partial meniscectomy is reserved for mechanical symptoms (true locking, persistent catching) that fail to improve with rehabilitation. Pain is managed with paracetamol, topical NSAIDs, and short courses of oral NSAIDs balanced against gastrointestinal and cardiovascular risk. Intra-articular corticosteroid injection provides 4-12 weeks of pain relief in degenerative tears. Platelet-rich plasma and hyaluronic acid injections have inconsistent evidence; biologic augmentation of meniscal repair (PRP, fibrin clot, mesenchymal stem cells) shows promise in early trials. Comprehensive rehabilitation includes quadriceps strengthening, gluteal activation, neuromuscular training, and sport-specific drills, with return to pivoting sport at 4-6 months after repair and 6-8 weeks after partial meniscectomy in most protocols.

medicalTreatments.name: Structured physical therapy (8-12 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Progressive program includes early range-of-motion, quadriceps and hamstring strengthening, gluteal activation, balance training, and sport-specific drills. Supervised in-clinic sessions twice weekly with home exercises.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 60-75% of degenerative tears achieve symptom relief equivalent to arthroscopic meniscectomy at 12 months in multiple randomized trials

medicalTreatments.name: Oral and topical NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400-600 mg three times daily, naproxen 500 mg twice daily, topical diclofenac)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line analgesia for acute pain and inflammation; topical options preferred in older patients to limit systemic toxicity. Short courses combined with paracetamol to spare gastric and cardiovascular risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction of 30-50% in acute and degenerative tear pain; topical NSAIDs equally effective with lower systemic risk

medicalTreatments.name: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection (triamcinolone 40 mg or methylprednisolone 40 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Useful for degenerative tears with significant pain not responding to oral analgesia. Injections under aseptic technique, often with ultrasound guidance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain relief in 60-70% of patients lasting 4-12 weeks; repeat injections at no more than 3-monthly intervals

medicalTreatments.name: Hinged or unloading knee brace
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces tibiofemoral compression in selected medial tears with early osteoarthritis. Patient-specific fit and adherence are critical.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction of 15-30% during weight-bearing activities; quality-of-life improvement in 50-60% of users

medicalTreatments.name: Activity modification and graduated return-to-sport program
medicalTreatments.description: Temporary cessation of cutting and pivoting sports; progressive return to running, cutting, and competition guided by functional milestones. Combined with neuromuscular training to reduce re-injury.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces re-injury risk by 30-40% versus unsupervised return; informs realistic recovery expectations

surgicalOptions.name: Arthroscopic meniscal repair (all-inside, inside-out, outside-in)
surgicalOptions.description: Sutures placed through the tear to allow biological healing in the peripheral red zone. Preferred for vertical longitudinal tears, root tears, and tears with adequate vascularity. Postoperative weight-bearing and motion restrictions for 6 weeks.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Tear healing in 70-90% of peripheral tears at 2 years; long-term reoperation rate 15-25%

surgicalOptions.name: Arthroscopic partial meniscectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Removes the unstable torn fragment while preserving as much meniscus as possible. Suitable for tears that cannot be repaired (white zone radial, complex, degenerative). Performed under regional or general anesthesia; recovery 2-6 weeks.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptom improvement in 80-90% of patients with mechanical symptoms; modest osteoarthritis acceleration at 10 years compared with intact meniscus

surgicalOptions.name: Meniscal root repair (transtibial pull-out)
surgicalOptions.description: Re-attaches an avulsed posterior or anterior root to the tibial plateau with transosseous sutures. Restores hoop tension and slows osteoarthritis progression.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Functional improvement in 75-85%; second-look healing in 60-70%; conversion to total knee replacement reduced by approximately 50% versus non-operative treatment

surgicalOptions.name: Meniscal allograft transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: Replacement of a previously excised meniscus with size-matched donor allograft. Indicated in young, active patients with prior subtotal meniscectomy and pain. Performed at specialist centers.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Functional and pain improvement in 75-85% of selected patients; graft survival 70-80% at 10 years

surgicalOptions.name: Knee arthroscopy with cartilage or ligament reconstruction (combined procedures)
surgicalOptions.description: Concurrent ACL reconstruction or cartilage restoration when associated injury is present. Reduces re-injury and improves long-term outcomes versus staged procedures.
surgicalOptions.successRate: ACL reconstruction with meniscal repair yields graft survival 85-95% and meniscal healing 70-85% at 5 years

alternativeTreatments.name: Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) augmentation
alternativeTreatments.description: Concentrated autologous platelets injected at meniscus repair sites or intra-articularly. Some evidence for improved healing rates in repair; standalone use for tears has inconsistent results in randomized trials.

alternativeTreatments.name: Hyaluronic acid intra-articular injection
alternativeTreatments.description: Used adjunctively in degenerative tears with early osteoarthritis. Modest pain relief over 3-6 months; less effective than corticosteroid short-term but with longer duration.

whySeeSpecialist: Suspected meniscus tear with mechanical symptoms (true locking, catching, recurrent giving way) or persistent pain after 4-6 weeks of conservative care warrants sports medicine or orthopedic referral. Acute traumatic tears in active patients benefit from early specialist assessment to maximize repair options, whereas degenerative tears in older patients should first complete a structured exercise program.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a sports medicine physician or orthopedic surgeon affiliated with a high-volume knee unit that performs both meniscal repair and complex ligament reconstruction. Volume-outcome data favor centers performing more than 100 meniscal procedures per year. Ask the surgeon about repair rates versus meniscectomy rates and long-term follow-up data.
hospitalCriteria: On-site MRI within 1-2 weeks of referral
Orthopedic and sports medicine outpatient clinic with knee specialists
Arthroscopy facilities for elective and urgent meniscal surgery
Physiotherapy and rehabilitation services with sport-specific protocols
Multidisciplinary cartilage and ligament reconstruction service for complex cases
keyFacilities: Cartilage restoration and meniscal transplant programs
preventionStrategies: Complete a neuromuscular training program (FIFA 11+, PEP) before and during pivoting sport seasons; reduces knee injuries by 30-40%.
Strengthen the quadriceps, hamstrings, and gluteal muscles year-round to support knee stability.
Use appropriate footwear and avoid worn cleats with excessive grip in pivoting sports.
Stretch and warm up for 10-15 minutes before vigorous activity; cold tissues tear more easily.
Modify or avoid deep squatting in occupational settings where possible; use kneeling pads and rotate duties.
Maintain body mass index under 30 kg/m² to reduce joint loading and degenerative tear risk.
lifestyleModifications: Adhere to the prescribed post-operative weight-bearing and range-of-motion restrictions for at least 6 weeks after repair.
Use crutches and a hinged brace as prescribed; avoid weight-bearing on uneven surfaces.
Ice the knee for 15-20 minutes 3-4 times daily for the first week to reduce swelling.
Elevate the leg above heart level when resting in the first 1-2 weeks.
Avoid pivoting, cutting, jumping, and contact sports until cleared by the surgeon and physical therapist.
Maintain a healthy weight to reduce loading on the recovering meniscus and surrounding cartilage.

recommended: Mediterranean-style diet with whole grains, vegetables, fruits, oily fish, and olive oil to reduce systemic inflammation
Adequate protein (1.2-1.6 g/kg/day) during rehabilitation to support muscle rebuilding
Calcium-rich foods and vitamin D 800-1000 IU daily for cartilage and bone health
Hydration with at least 2 L of water daily
avoid: Excessive alcohol that impairs collagen synthesis and tendon healing
Sugar-sweetened beverages and ultra-processed foods that promote weight gain and joint inflammation
Smoking (nicotine impairs cartilage and ligament healing)
Very high-protein supplementation without medical advice in chronic kidney disease

exerciseGuidelines: Initial 6 weeks after repair: range-of-motion within bracing limits, quadriceps activation, partial weight-bearing. Weeks 6-12: progressive resistance training, closed-chain exercises, balance and proprioception. Weeks 12-16: jogging and sport-specific drills. Return to pivoting sport at 4-6 months after repair, 6-8 weeks after meniscectomy, guided by quadriceps strength symmetry and hop testing.
dailyManagement: Perform prescribed home exercise program at least 5 days per week.
Use the brace or sleeve during weight-bearing activity as prescribed.
Apply ice and compression after activity in the first 4-6 weeks.
Take prescribed analgesia (paracetamol, NSAIDs) at scheduled times rather than waiting for pain.
Keep a symptom diary noting pain, swelling, locking, and giving-way episodes.
Attend physical therapy appointments and progress assessments on the agreed schedule.
prognosis: Outcomes depend on tear type, location, treatment, and adherence to rehabilitation. Peripheral vertical tears in young athletes treated with arthroscopic repair achieve 70-90% healing rates at 2 years; return to pre-injury sport is 80-90% by 6 months. Partial meniscectomy provides reliable short-term symptomatic relief in 80-90% of mechanical tears but accelerates joint-space narrowing over 5-10 years, with a 30-40% risk of symptomatic osteoarthritis at 15 years. Degenerative tears managed non-operatively have equivalent functional outcomes to surgical management at 1, 2, and 5 years in randomized trials. Root tear repair reduces conversion to knee replacement by approximately 50% versus non-operative treatment in observational cohorts. Long-term, knee replacement rates are 4-5 times higher after subtotal meniscectomy than after intact or repaired menisci; meniscal preservation is therefore strongly preferred in young patients.
recoveryTimeline: Arthroscopic partial meniscectomy: walking without crutches in 1-2 weeks, return to office work in 1-2 weeks, recreational running in 4-6 weeks, pivoting sport in 6-8 weeks. Meniscal repair: protected weight-bearing for 4-6 weeks, full weight-bearing by 8 weeks, jogging at 3-4 months, return to pivoting sport at 4-6 months. Root repair: weight-bearing restrictions for 6-8 weeks, return to sport at 6-9 months.
complications: Persistent or recurrent symptoms after surgery (re-tear, failed repair) in 10-25% of repaired menisci over 5 years; second-look arthroscopy may be required.
Accelerated osteoarthritis after partial or total meniscectomy; risk of total knee replacement increases 4-5 fold over 15 years versus an intact meniscus.
Surgical site infection in 0.5-1% of arthroscopic procedures; managed with washout and antibiotics.
Venous thromboembolism after knee surgery — symptomatic deep vein thrombosis 1-2%, pulmonary embolism 0.1-0.2%; prophylaxis with mobilization and risk-adjusted anticoagulation.
Stiffness and arthrofibrosis, especially after combined procedures; managed with intensive rehabilitation and occasionally manipulation under anesthesia.
Common peroneal nerve injury or saphenous nerve neuropraxia in 1-2% of meniscal repairs.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons (AAOS) — Meniscus Tears
supportResources.description: Patient-focused guide on meniscus tears, surgical and non-surgical options, and rehabilitation timelines.

supportResources.name: American Orthopaedic Society for Sports Medicine
supportResources.description: Sports medicine guidance on meniscus injury, return to play, and prevention.

supportResources.name: ESSKA Meniscus Consensus
supportResources.description: European clinical consensus on meniscus tears including degenerative tear management.

supportResources.name: NICE Guidance on knee arthroscopy
supportResources.description: UK National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guidance on meniscal surgery indications.

confusedWithConditions.name: Osteoarthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Knee osteoarthritis causes gradual pain, stiffness, and crepitus over months to years, with X-ray joint-space narrowing and osteophytes. Meniscus tears cause sharper pain at the joint line and mechanical symptoms (locking, catching), and MRI shows a discrete tear. The two often coexist in older patients.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: RA causes symmetric joint inflammation with morning stiffness over 30 minutes, raised inflammatory markers, and anti-CCP positivity. Meniscus tear is asymmetric, mechanical, and typically follows injury or develops insidiously in one knee; inflammatory markers are normal.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gout
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute gout causes sudden severe pain, warmth, redness, and swelling within 24 hours, often in the big toe but also in the knee. Synovial fluid analysis shows urate crystals. Meniscus tear evolves with mechanical symptoms after specific injury and is diagnosed by MRI.

confusedWithConditions.name: Plantar Fasciitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Plantar fasciitis causes heel pain on first steps in the morning and improves with activity; meniscus tear causes knee joint-line pain that worsens with twisting and squatting. Examination and imaging readily distinguish the two.

relatedConditions.name: Osteoarthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Degenerative meniscal tears often coexist with early knee osteoarthritis and share management strategies focused on exercise therapy.

relatedConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: RA can cause meniscal damage through chronic inflammation; rheumatologic management modifies surgical decisions.

relatedConditions.name: Gout
relatedConditions.relevance: Acute knee pain differential and may complicate post-arthroscopy course; identification prevents inappropriate antibiotic use for what appears to be septic arthritis.

relatedConditions.name: Plantar Fasciitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Common in patients with altered gait after knee injury; addressed in comprehensive rehabilitation.

faqs.question: What is a meniscus tear in simple terms?
faqs.answer: A meniscus tear is a rip in the C-shaped cartilage cushion between the thigh and shin bones in the knee. Tears can happen from a twisting injury during sport or gradually with age. They cause pain on the joint line, swelling, and sometimes a feeling of the knee locking.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of a torn meniscus?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include sharp pain on the inner or outer side of the knee, swelling that develops over 6-24 hours, catching or clicking with movement, true locking (inability to fully straighten), and pain on squatting or twisting. Mechanical symptoms strongly suggest a tear.

faqs.question: How do you tell if you have a meniscus tear?
faqs.answer: A medical history with a twisting injury, joint-line tenderness, positive McMurray or Thessaly test, and persistent mechanical symptoms suggest a tear. MRI is the imaging gold standard, with sensitivity of 90-95% for medial tears. Arthroscopy is used when imaging is equivocal.

faqs.question: Can a meniscus tear heal on its own?
faqs.answer: Yes, in selected cases. Small peripheral tears in the vascular outer third can heal with conservative care over 6-12 weeks. White-zone tears in the avascular inner two-thirds rarely heal without surgery. Degenerative tears typically do not heal but symptoms often improve with exercise therapy.

faqs.question: Do I need surgery for a meniscus tear?
faqs.answer: Not always. Acute traumatic tears in young athletes often benefit from arthroscopic repair to preserve the meniscus. Degenerative tears in middle-aged adults respond as well to structured physical therapy as to arthroscopic surgery. Surgery is reserved for mechanical locking or symptoms unresponsive to 3-6 months of rehabilitation.

faqs.question: What is the difference between meniscus repair and meniscectomy?
faqs.answer: Meniscus repair uses sutures to allow the tear to heal in the vascular outer rim, preserving meniscal tissue and protecting against osteoarthritis. Meniscectomy removes the torn fragment when repair is not possible. Repair has slower recovery but better long-term outcomes in suitable tears.

faqs.question: How long does meniscus surgery recovery take?
faqs.answer: Arthroscopic partial meniscectomy: walking without crutches in 1-2 weeks, return to office work in 1-2 weeks, recreational running in 4-6 weeks, pivoting sport in 6-8 weeks. Meniscal repair: protected weight-bearing for 4-6 weeks, return to pivoting sport at 4-6 months.

faqs.question: Will a meniscus tear cause arthritis?
faqs.answer: Total or subtotal meniscectomy accelerates joint-space narrowing over 5-10 years and increases knee replacement rates 4-5 fold versus an intact meniscus. Repair preserves meniscal tissue and reduces osteoarthritis risk by approximately 30% at 10 years compared with meniscectomy.

faqs.question: What does it feel like when your meniscus is torn?
faqs.answer: Patients describe sharp pain on the joint line during pivoting, squatting, or twisting, swelling over 6-24 hours, catching or clicking, and sometimes true locking of the knee. Pain often worsens with deep flexion and stairs. Mild aching may continue for weeks or months in degenerative tears.

faqs.question: How long does it take to recover from a meniscus tear without surgery?
faqs.answer: Conservative care for stable peripheral tears or degenerative tears involves 6-12 weeks of physical therapy with progressive return to activity. Up to 70% of patients with degenerative tears experience meaningful symptom improvement; mechanical locking that fails to respond may need surgery.

faqs.question: Can you walk on a torn meniscus?
faqs.answer: Many people can walk on a torn meniscus with limp or discomfort. True mechanical locking that prevents knee extension makes walking very difficult and warrants urgent orthopedic review. Walking on an unstable knee risks further damage to cartilage and the meniscus.

faqs.question: How is a bucket-handle meniscus tear treated?
faqs.answer: Bucket-handle tears displace centrally into the knee and cause mechanical locking. Treatment is urgent arthroscopic reduction and repair within 6 weeks of injury when possible. Repair preserves the meniscus; partial meniscectomy is performed when tissue quality precludes repair.

faqs.question: What is the McMurray test?
faqs.answer: The McMurray test is a bedside maneuver in which the knee is fully flexed and then extended while the tibia is rotated internally and externally. A palpable click or reproducible pain at the joint line supports a meniscal tear. Sensitivity is 60-70%; specificity 70-95%.

faqs.question: Can a meniscus tear heal without locking?
faqs.answer: Yes. Stable peripheral tears and small degenerative tears often improve without surgery through inflammation resolution and adapted activity. True locking from displaced fragments typically requires arthroscopic intervention to release the knee and address the tear.

faqs.question: Is meniscus surgery painful?
faqs.answer: Arthroscopic meniscus surgery is usually performed under regional or general anesthesia and produces moderate pain that responds to multimodal analgesia (paracetamol, NSAIDs, occasional short-course opioids). Most patients return to office work within 1-2 weeks after meniscectomy.

faqs.question: Can a meniscus tear cause a Baker's cyst?
faqs.answer: Yes. A meniscus tear increases synovial fluid production and can produce a popliteal (Baker's) cyst that bulges behind the knee. Treating the underlying tear and inflammation often reduces the cyst; persistent cysts may require ultrasound-guided aspiration.

faqs.question: What activities should I avoid after a meniscus tear?
faqs.answer: Avoid pivoting and cutting sports, deep squatting, kneeling, jumping, and twisting until cleared by the surgeon and physical therapist. Maintain straight-line activities (walking, swimming, stationary cycling) within tolerance during early recovery.

faqs.question: Are PRP injections effective for meniscus tears?
faqs.answer: Platelet-rich plasma has shown promise in augmenting meniscus repair surgery; standalone PRP injections for tears have inconsistent evidence in randomized trials. Some patients report symptom improvement, but PRP is not yet considered standard care for isolated meniscus tears.

faqs.question: How do I prevent meniscus tears in sport?
faqs.answer: Complete a neuromuscular training program (FIFA 11+, PEP) before and during the season, strengthen quadriceps and hamstrings, warm up for 10-15 minutes before activity, wear appropriate footwear, and progress training intensity gradually. These measures reduce knee injuries by 30-40%.

faqs.question: Can a meniscus tear be diagnosed on X-ray?
faqs.answer: No. X-rays do not show meniscal cartilage. They exclude fractures and assess joint-space narrowing and osteoarthritis. MRI is the imaging test of choice for meniscal tears with sensitivity of 90-95% for medial and 80-85% for lateral tears.

faqs.question: Do I need to wear a brace after meniscus surgery?
faqs.answer: After meniscal repair, a hinged knee brace is usually worn for 4-6 weeks with restricted flexion and protected weight-bearing. After partial meniscectomy, a brace is optional and depends on associated injuries and patient comfort. Adherence to brace and weight-bearing instructions protects healing.

metaTitle: Meniscus Tear: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Meniscus tear: knee cartilage injury causing pain, swelling and locking. Diagnosis, surgery vs physical therapy, and recovery, medically reviewed.
